thesis_status_reasoning: Multiple falsification criteria are either breached or critically close to breach. Pumps 1 and 2 are operating at 128.5% and 131% of rated capacity (exceeding the 115% Redundant Load Threshold), with vibration at 45 Hz and 48.5 Hz respectively (exceeding the 35 Hz Cavitation Threshold while load >95%). The Divergent Inventory Paradox is partially manifesting: reservoir is at 96% while only two of four pumps can maintain barely-adequate 42 PSI system pressure. Fire dispatch has independently confirmed hydraulic insufficiency in Sector B by deploying water tanker protocols, validating that the system cannot meet its life-safety mandate under current conditions.
action_reasoning: Continued operation will cause irreversible cavitation damage to the remaining two functional pumps within hours, leaving zero redundancy and precipitating complete system collapse. Immediate controlled shutdown preserves pump assets for strategic restart after emergency repairs to Pump 3 and load rebalancing.

key_signals.signal: Pumps 1 and 2 operating at 128.5% and 131% load with vibration at 45 Hz and 48.5 Hz
Fire department deploying water tankers to Sector B due to insufficient hydrant pressure
System pressure at 42 PSI, only 2 PSI above regulatory minimum despite 96% reservoir level
Pump 3 tripped due to overtemp with cavitation detected, Pump 4 offline for maintenance
Maintenance log explicitly confirms cavitation in Pumps 1 and 2

biggest_uncertainty: Time remaining until catastrophic mechanical failure of Pumps 1 and 2 under current cavitation conditions, which determines whether controlled shutdown can prevent total pumping capacity loss.
